tags: Podcast episode: Ray County Commissioner's Meeting 4/29

The County Commission meeting opened with an invocation and the recognition of Child Abuse Prevention Day. During public comments, citizens raised concerns regarding road maintenance, specifically ditch issues on Aikenwood Road, and procedures for submitting Sunshine Law requests. The Commission confirmed that old request rates would be honored.

Key administrative actions included the approval of previous meeting minutes, payables, and a plat for Tucker Trails. The Commission also voted to waive irregularities and consider a single late bid for the Holt College project.

A significant portion of the meeting featured an update from Great River Engineering. They discussed the Myers Road Bridge design, which now features a vertical curve and deep pile foundations. For the West 216th Street Bridge, the commission heard plans to utilize precast boxes installed by a contractor. Engineering estimates for the West 84th Street Bridge replacement were significantly higher than anticipated due to the required structure size.

The board also discussed the Lexington-Henrietta bridge, exploring the option of removing only the superstructure to save costs and limit liability near railroad tracks, potentially earning "soft match" credit for future federal funding.

===== FULL DESCRIPTION =====

# County Commission Meeting Summary

## Meeting Overview
The county commission convened for a regular session that included public comments, approval of minutes and payables, planning and zoning matters, and extensive discussions regarding bridge and road projects throughout the county.

## Opening Proceedings
The meeting began with an invocation. Commissioners and attendees were dressed in blue in recognition of Child Abuse Awareness Day, with a ceremony scheduled for 11:30 AM. Julie was recognized for her efforts decorating the county grounds for the occasion.

## Public Comments

### Road and Drainage Issues
A resident raised concerns about ongoing drainage problems on Aikenwood Road near Raymoor and Clevenger. The citizen reported that repeated applications of rock have washed away during rains, plugging a drainage pipe and making driveway access difficult. The resident, a retired road worker with approximately 30 years of experience, suggested the county's approach of accelerating water flow was counterproductive and requested assistance with pipe replacement. The issue was acknowledged, and county staff agreed to examine the situation.

### Road Complaint Reporting System
A citizen announced the launch of f3oads.com (where "f" stands for "fix"), a website allowing residents to report road issues. Reports would be routed to appropriate special road districts and county officials, with an open map tracking submitted issues.

However, commissioners indicated preference for a county-controlled system. Two citizens had prepared a Google Doc form for submission through the official county website. Commissioners agreed that an official county-sanctioned form should be the primary reporting mechanism rather than external platforms.

### Sunshine Requests
Discussion addressed changes to Sunshine Request fees under the new custodian of record. Commissioners confirmed the county would honor previous rates for requests submitted before the fee change. Citizens with unfulfilled requests were advised to resubmit them. All Sunshine Requests must be paid individually; the county does not offer pre-funded accounts.

## Administrative Actions

### Minutes and Payables
Commissioners voted to untable and approve minutes from the 22nd and 23rd, followed by approval of payables. All motions passed unanimously.

### Planning and Zoning
A plat for Tucker Razors was approved via motion. One commissioner recused themselves from this vote.

A progress report for Myers was presented but held for further review due to missing signatures.

## Engineering Report: Great River Representatives

### Consultant Selection Process
Great River representatives provided detailed information on qualification-based selection for engineering services, referencing both the federal Brooks Act and Missouri's mini-Brooks Act. For projects using federal funds, qualification-based selection is mandatory. The process varies by contract size: under $200,000 in engineering fees allows selection from a MoDOT-approved consultant list, while contracts exceeding $200,000 require a formal Request for Qualifications process.

### Myers Road Bridge
The Myers Road Bridge project remains in the design phase. Engineers added a vertical curve to the bridge, reducing approach grades from an originally planned six to eight feet of elevation to approximately five feet. The substructure design involves cast-in-place walls with pile footings extending 20 to 25 feet to reach solid rock, necessary because subsurface conditions consist of unstable glacial till.

Temporary construction easements will likely be needed from two property owners: Joni Myers and Sharon Myers, and Carl Winkle and Mary Van Winkle. Final plans are expected to be submitted in June or July, with construction bidding to follow and work potentially beginning next spring.

### West 216th Street Bridge
This bridge replacement will utilize precast box culverts creating two 12-foot lanes with headwalls at the inlet and outlet. Commissioners clarified that county forces lack equipment to set the precast structures, requiring a contractor for installation. The county may perform backfill and finishing work.

The project sits within a 60-foot right-of-way corridor. Formal easements may not be necessary if property owners provide verbal permission for access and rock placement in the creek.

### West 84th Street Bridge
Survey revealed significantly different conditions than initially anticipated, with the channel approximately 18 feet deep. This necessitates a structure spanning roughly 78 to 78.5 feet using precast NU girders.

Engineers presented comparable bidding data from a similar Monitee County project, showing construction bids ranging from approximately $410,000 to $682,000. The estimated construction cost for this project is $500,000 to $600,000, with engineering costs of $130,000. Commissioners were advised that bids could come in lower depending on contractor demand.

The project will require coordination with the railroad, though statutory right-of-way exists. Engineers noted that railroads typically require formal agreements even when existing rights are present.

### Lexington Avenue/Henrietta Street Bridge
Commissioners discussed the planned demolition of this bridge structure. MoDOT allows demolition to count toward BRO requirements, and the county may receive soft match credit for the work.

Engineers presented two options: full demolition of the superstructure and substructure, or removal of only the superstructure. The substructure appears stable and could potentially remain for decades. Partial demolition would roughly halve the cost and significantly reduce safety risks associated with excavation near active railroad tracks, which requires shoring and protective measures.

Commissioners expressed concerns about liability and cost, indicating preference for removing only the superstructure at this time.

### Bridge BRO Applications
Great River reminded commissioners that MoDOT's next call for BRO projects was expected imminently. The county must identify its top two bridge priorities for applications. Bridge ratings derive from inspections, with structures scoring 4 or below on a 1-to-10 scale qualifying for the list. Commissioners requested the updated list before making selections.

## Holt College Bid
Via phone, officials requested commissioners waive an irregularity regarding a single bid received for the Holt College project, which arrived one hour past the deadline. The county attorney recommended approval given that only one bidder responded. Commissioners approved the motion to waive the irregularity and review the bid for consideration.

## Closing Items
Commissioners noted the Eagleton Agreement regarding rental changes would appear on the following week's agenda. The meeting then transitioned to closed session.
